content.text: What a Policy Exemption Is

A policy exemption is an ARM resource (Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions) that overrides the compliance state of a specific scope or resource for a specific policy assignment. When an exemption is active, the policy engine still evaluates the resource against the policy, but the resulting compliance state is recorded as Exempt rather than NonCompliant. Exempt resources are excluded from the compliance percentage denominator, so they neither help nor hurt the overall compliance score.

attrs.alt: Policy exemption scope, categories (Waiver/Mitigated), and lifecycle (create → active → review → expired → delete)

content.text: Exemptions are the correct tool when a resource has a legitimate reason to deviate from a policy — either because the control does not apply (Waiver) or because an alternative control satisfies the same requirement (Mitigated). They are not a workaround for misconfigured policies or a way to silence noisy compliance dashboards without audit trail.
Exemption vs notScopes vs Disabled Effect
Mechanism
Evaluation?
Compliance State
Scope Granularity
Audit Trail
Exemption (Waiver/Mitigated)
Yes — still evaluated
Exempt
Any ARM resource, subscription, or RG
Category + description + expiry stored on ARM resource
notScopes on assignment
No — skipped entirely
Not evaluated
Resource group or subscription paths only
Listed on the assignment — no per-resource tracking
Effect=Disabled
No — policy skipped
Not Started
Entire assignment scope
Only visible if you inspect assignment parameters

Use exemptions for individual resources or small groups with documented justification. Use notScopes to exclude entire environments (e.g., a test subscription or a specific resource group). Use Disabled only to turn off a policy effect entirely for a given assignment scope.
Exemption Categories
Azure Policy defines two exemption categories. The category is a required field and affects how auditors interpret the exemption in a compliance review.
Category
Meaning
When to use
Expiry
Waiver
The policy control is intentionally not being followed. Risk is accepted by the resource owner.
Legacy resources that cannot be upgraded, short-term exceptions during migrations, resources with compensating controls outside Azure
Required — forces periodic review
Mitigated
The policy's underlying requirement IS satisfied, but through a different mechanism that Azure Policy cannot detect.
Resources protected by a third-party firewall, resources with manual encryption that don't use Azure's encryption keys, resources using a SIEM that isn't Log Analytics
Recommended but not required
Creating Exemptions (Azure CLI)

# Exempt a specific resource from an assignment (Waiver — legacy OS)
az policy exemption create \
  --name exempt-vm-legacy-law-agent \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01 \
  --policy-assignment /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-law-agent \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --description 'vm-legacy-01 runs Windows Server 2008 R2 which does not support the Log Analytics Agent (MMA). End-of-life Q1 2027.' \
  --expires-on 2027-03-31T00:00:00Z

# Exempt a resource group from an initiative assignment — targets the whole RG
az policy exemption create \
  --name exempt-rg-sandbox \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-sandbox \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/corp-baseline \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --description 'Sandbox RG — used for experimental workloads that are not subject to production policy.' \
  --expires-on 2026-12-31T00:00:00Z

# Exempt from a specific policy within an initiative (not all policies)
az policy exemption create \
  --name exempt-vm-legacy-agent-only \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01 \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/corp-baseline \
  --policy-definition-reference-ids auditVmLawAgent deployKvDiag \
  --exemption-category Mitigated \
  --description 'vm-legacy-01 monitored by legacy SIEM (Splunk HEC). LA agent not applicable.' \
  --expires-on 2026-06-30T00:00:00Z

# View all active exemptions under a management group
az policy exemption list \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Scope:id,Category:exemptionCategory,Expiry:expiresOn}' \
  -o table
Terraform: Policy Exemptions

# Waiver exemption for a legacy VM
resource "azurerm_resource_policy_exemption" "vm_legacy_01" {
  name                 = "exempt-vm-legacy-law-agent"
  resource_id          = "/subscriptions/${var.sub_id}/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01"
  policy_assignment_id = azurerm_subscription_policy_assignment.deploy_law.id
  exemption_category   = "Waiver"
  description          = "vm-legacy-01 runs Windows Server 2008 R2 — MMA agent not supported. EOL Q1 2027."
  expires_on           = "2027-03-31T00:00:00Z"
}

# Mitigated exemption for a resource group with an alternative SIEM
resource "azurerm_resource_group_policy_exemption" "rg_legacy_siem" {
  name                 = "exempt-rg-splunk-monitored"
  resource_group_id    = "/subscriptions/${var.sub_id}/resourceGroups/rg-splunk-env"
  policy_assignment_id = var.corp_baseline_assignment_id
  exemption_category   = "Mitigated"
  description          = "Resource group monitored by on-premise Splunk via HEC. LA agent not required."

  # Target only the specific policy references within the initiative
  # Leave other policies in the initiative still enforced
  policy_definition_reference_ids = [
    "auditVmLawAgent",
    "deployKvDiag"
  ]
}

# Exemption with no expiry (Mitigated — long-term alternative control)
resource "azurerm_management_group_policy_exemption" "mg_nist_mitigated" {
  name                 = "exempt-mg-nist-alt-control"
  management_group_id  = var.corp_mg_id
  policy_assignment_id = var.nist_assignment_id
  exemption_category   = "Mitigated"
  description          = "Physical access controls documented in ISO 27001 audit. NIST PE controls not applicable to Azure-only scope."
  # No expires_on — permanent until manually reviewed
}
Managing Exemption Lifecycle at Scale

Exemptions accumulate over time, especially in large tenants. Without active lifecycle management, an organization ends up with hundreds of expired or unnecessary exemptions that distort the compliance score and create audit risk. A quarterly exemption review process using Resource Graph is the standard approach.

# Find exemptions that are already expired
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions'
| where isnotempty(properties.expiresOn)
| where todatetime(properties.expiresOn) < now()
| project
    name        = name,
    scope       = id,
    expiredOn   = todatetime(properties.expiresOn),
    category    = tostring(properties.exemptionCategory),
    description = tostring(properties.description)
| order by expiredOn asc
" --management-groups mg-corp

# Find exemptions expiring in the next 30 days
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions'
| where isnotempty(properties.expiresOn)
| where todatetime(properties.expiresOn) between (now() .. now() + 30d)
| project name, scope = id, expiresOn = todatetime(properties.expiresOn), category = tostring(properties.exemptionCategory)
| order by expiresOn asc
" --management-groups mg-corp

# Delete an expired exemption that is no longer needed
az policy exemption delete \
  --name exempt-vm-legacy-law-agent \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01

# Renew an exemption (update the expiry date)
az policy exemption update \
  --name exempt-vm-legacy-law-agent \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01 \
  --expires-on 2028-06-30T00:00:00Z

# Count active exemptions by category across the tenant
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions'
| where isempty(properties.expiresOn) or todatetime(properties.expiresOn) > now()
| summarize count() by category = tostring(properties.exemptionCategory)
" --management-groups mg-corp
Exemption Scope and Inheritance

Exemptions are scoped to the resource path you specify. An exemption created at a resource group scope exempts the group and all resources beneath it from the named assignment or initiative. An exemption created at a subscription scope cascades down to all resource groups and resources in that subscription. The most common mistake is creating an exemption at the wrong scope and wondering why a specific resource is still NonCompliant.

For initiative assignments, a top-level exemption without policyDefinitionReferenceIds exempts the resource from every policy inside the initiative. Specifying policyDefinitionReferenceIds limits the exemption to only those controls — the resource remains evaluated (and potentially NonCompliant) for all other policies in the initiative. This makes per-control exemptions surgical and auditable.

# Scope hierarchy for exemptions:
# Management Group > Subscription > Resource Group > Resource

# Exempt an entire subscription from an MG-level assignment
az policy exemption create \
  --name exempt-legacy-sub \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/corp-baseline \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --description 'Legacy subscription pending migration — approved 2026-Q1'

# Exempt a resource group from the same assignment
az policy exemption create \
  --name exempt-rg-dev \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-dev-sandbox \
  --policy-assignment /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/corp-baseline \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --expires-on 2026-12-31T23:59:00Z

# Check what exemptions apply to a resource (including inherited ones)
az policy exemption list \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-dev-sandbox/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-test \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Category:exemptionCategory,Scope:id,Expiry:expiresOn,Assignment:policyAssignmentId}' \
  -o table

# List ALL exemptions across a management group (flattened)
az policy exemption list \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query 'sort_by([].{Name:name,Category:exemptionCategory,Scope:id,Expiry:expiresOn}, &Expiry)' \
  -o table

# Delete an exemption
az policy exemption delete \
  --name exempt-legacy-sub \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID
Terraform: Managing Exemptions as Code

Storing exemptions in Terraform provides the same review workflow as policy assignments — PRs for new exemptions, audit history in git, expiry enforced by resource definition. The three azurerm_*_policy_exemption resources map directly to resource, resource group, and management group scope.

# Variables
variable "corp_mg_id" { type = string }
variable "sub_id"     { type = string }

# 1. Resource-level exemption (Mitigated — alternative control in place)
resource "azurerm_resource_policy_exemption" "vm_legacy_agent" {
  name                 = "exempt-vm-legacy-no-mma"
  resource_id          = "/subscriptions/${var.sub_id}/resourceGroups/rg-legacy/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-legacy-01"
  policy_assignment_id = azurerm_management_group_policy_assignment.deploy_mma.id
  exemption_category   = "Mitigated"
  description          = "AMA agent deployed instead — workbook confirmed coverage"
  expires_on           = "2026-12-31T23:59:00Z"
}

# 2. Resource group exemption (Waiver — dev sandbox excluded from prod controls)
resource "azurerm_resource_group_policy_exemption" "dev_sandbox" {
  name                 = "exempt-rg-dev-sandbox"
  resource_group_id    = "/subscriptions/${var.sub_id}/resourceGroups/rg-dev-sandbox"
  policy_assignment_id = azurerm_management_group_policy_assignment.corp_baseline.id
  exemption_category   = "Waiver"
  description          = "Dev sandbox — approved to bypass corp-baseline controls"
  expires_on           = "2026-06-30T23:59:00Z"

  # Only waive two controls; remain evaluated for everything else
  policy_definition_reference_ids = [
    "requireDiagnosticsSettings",
    "requirePrivateEndpoint"
  ]
}

# 3. Management group exemption (Waiver — whole child MG excluded from initiative)
resource "azurerm_management_group_policy_exemption" "sandbox_mg" {
  name                    = "exempt-mg-sandbox"
  management_group_id     = "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-sandbox"
  policy_assignment_id    = azurerm_management_group_policy_assignment.corp_baseline.id
  exemption_category      = "Waiver"
  description             = "Sandbox MG — compliance tracking disabled by design"
  expires_on              = "2026-12-31T23:59:00Z"
}

output "dev_sandbox_exemption_id" {
  value = azurerm_resource_group_policy_exemption.dev_sandbox.id
}
Exemption Governance: Preventing Exemption Sprawl

Exemptions without governance become a compliance debt sink. Unreviewed Waiver exemptions accumulate over time, each one hiding resources from the compliance score. Effective governance requires an expiry on every exemption, a review workflow before creation, and automated alerting as expiry approaches so resources are not silently re-evaluated after the exemption lapses.

# Resource Graph: find all exemptions expiring in the next 30 days
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions'
| where isnotempty(properties.expiresOn)
| where todatetime(properties.expiresOn) between (now() .. now() + 30d)
| project
    name       = name,
    scope      = id,
    category   = tostring(properties.exemptionCategory),
    assignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentId),
    expiresOn  = todatetime(properties.expiresOn)
| order by expiresOn asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Resource Graph: count exemptions by category across the MG hierarchy
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions'
| summarize
    Total    = count(),
    Waivers  = countif(tostring(properties.exemptionCategory) == 'Waiver'),
    Mitigated = countif(tostring(properties.exemptionCategory) == 'Mitigated'),
    Expired  = countif(todatetime(properties.expiresOn) < now())
    by sub = subscriptionId
| order by Waivers desc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Resource Graph: find exemptions with NO expiry (bad practice — should always expire)
az graph query -q "
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyExemptions'
| where isempty(properties.expiresOn)
| project
    name       = name,
    scope      = id,
    category   = tostring(properties.exemptionCategory),
    assignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentId)
| order by category asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Audit trail: check who created an exemption (Activity Log)
az monitor activity-log list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --resource-provider Microsoft.Authorization \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '7 days ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ 2>/dev/null || date -u -v-7d +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --query "[?contains(operationName.value,'exemptions')].{Time:eventTimestamp,Op:operationName.value,Caller:caller,Status:status.value}" \
  -o table
Exemptions vs notScopes vs Disabled Effect: Decision Guide

All three mechanisms can hide a resource from a policy, but they operate at different layers and leave different audit trails. Choosing the wrong mechanism makes compliance reports misleading and creates security gaps that are hard to detect during audits.
Mechanism
Where Configured
Granularity
Visible in Compliance Report
When to Use
Policy Exemption
Separate resource on the resource/RG/Sub/MG
Per-resource, per-policy-reference, with expiry and category
Yes — shown as Exempt state with reason
Specific resource needs temporary or permanent bypass; full audit trail required
notScopes on assignment
Inside the policy assignment definition
Entire scope tree (sub, RG) — cannot target individual resources
Resources in notScopes show as Not Started or excluded from count
Entire environment tier (dev, test) that should never be evaluated
Disabled effect parameter
Effect parameter value on the policy definition or assignment
Entire policy / initiative across all resources
All resources show as Not Started — entire policy stops evaluating
Emergency bypass of a broken policy; staged rollout (evaluate before enforce)

# Check what notScopes are configured on an assignment
az policy assignment show \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --query 'notScopes' -o json

# Add a notScope to an existing assignment (replaces the entire notScopes array)
CURRENT=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name corp-baseline --management-group mg-corp --query 'notScopes' -o json)
NEW_SCOPE='/subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-exempted'
UPDATED=$(echo $CURRENT | python3 -c "import json,sys; l=json.load(sys.stdin); l.append('$NEW_SCOPE'); print(json.dumps(l))")
az policy assignment update \
  --name corp-baseline \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --not-scopes $UPDATED

# Disable a specific policy in an initiative via effect parameter override
# (policy must expose an 'effect' parameter with 'Disabled' as an allowed value)
az policy assignment create \
  --name corp-baseline-with-disable \
  --management-group mg-corp \
  --policy-set-definition /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/corp-baseline \
  --params '{
    "requireDiagSettings_effect": { "value": "Disabled" }
  }'
Common Exemption Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Correct Approach
Creating an exemption without an expiry date
Exemption never triggers review; resources stay hidden indefinitely
Always set --expires-on; use a maximum 1-year window for Waiver, 3-months for emergency Waivers
Using Waiver when Mitigated is correct
Compliance report misrepresents the actual security posture — appears to be intentional bypass rather than alternative control
Use Mitigated when an equivalent compensating control is in place; reserve Waiver for true policy non-applicability
Exempting at subscription scope instead of resource scope
Hundreds of resources are hidden from compliance tracking when only one needed the exemption
Target the most specific scope — prefer resource or resource group over subscription or MG
Not using policyDefinitionReferenceIds for initiative exemptions
All controls in the initiative are bypassed even though only one was non-applicable
Specify policyDefinitionReferenceIds to limit exemption to the specific control(s) needed
Exempting instead of fixing the policy
Technical debt accumulates; the exemption masks a broken existenceCondition or wrong policy logic
Investigate why the resource is NonCompliant first; fix the policy if the logic is wrong
Summary

Policy exemptions override the compliance state of a resource from NonCompliant to Exempt for a specific policy assignment. Use Waiver when the control is intentionally not followed, and Mitigated when an alternative control satisfies the same requirement. Target exemptions to individual policies within an initiative using policyDefinitionReferenceIds. Always set an expiry date and review exemptions quarterly using Resource Graph queries to identify expired or stale exemptions that should be removed or renewed.
